original_abstract:
Maintaining water quality is essential for human health, aquatic biodiversity, and industrial sustainability. In recent decades, increasing levels of environmental pollution have necessitated the development of advanced monitoring systems. This study addresses the challenge of accurately evaluating the Water Quality Index (WQI) and Water Quality Classification (WQC) through optimized machine learning frameworks. Using a dataset of 1,991 instances featuring seven key parameters—including pH, dissolved oxygen, and biological oxygen demand—the research employs rigorous preprocessing steps such as mean imputation and data normalization.

To enhance predictive performance, a grid search algorithm was integrated with cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning across eight different models. For the classification of water quality status, four ensemble methods were evaluated: Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Gradient Boosting (GB), and Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost). For the regression-based estimation of WQI values, four techniques were tested: K-nearest neighbors (KNN), decision trees (DT), support vector regression (SVR), and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) networks. The models were validated using a comprehensive suite of metrics, including accuracy, F1 score, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for classification, alongside Mean Square Error (MSE) and R-squared (R²) for regression.

Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized Gradient Boosting model achieved the highest classification performance with an accuracy of 99.50%. In the regression analysis, the MLP regressor significantly outperformed other methods, attaining an R² value of 99.8%. These findings suggest that the integration of grid search optimization with ensemble and neural network architectures provides a highly reliable and efficient approach for real-time water quality assessment and environmental management.
humanized_abstract:
Water quality management is a critical factor for human health, biodiversity and industrial production. Nowadays due to a high level of contamination in the environment, monitoring systems have to be more precise. This study investigates methods for determining the optimal Water Quality Index (WQI) and Water Quality Classification (WQC) by applying the most accurate machine learning algorithms. Thus, a comprehensive dataset comprising 1,991 samples, which were obtained from seven variables (pH, DO, BOD, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+ and HCO3-), was prepared and improved through mean imputation and normalization processes.

In order to train and evaluate the models more effectively, a grid search with cross-validation strategy was employed on eight models to observe the improvement in their accuracy. Four ensemble machine learning models were used to classify water quality status: Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Gradient Boosting (GB), and Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost). Four more regression based machine learning models were tested to predict WQI values: K-nearest neighbors (KNN), decision trees (DT), support vector regression (SVR), and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) networks. All models were evaluated by an array of evaluation metrics: accuracy, F1 score, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for classification and Mean Square Error (MSE) and R-squared (R²) for regression.

A 99.50% classification accuracy was obtained from the Gradient Boosting model which we optimized via experimental testing. For the regression, the best performance was obtained by our MLP regressor model with R² = 99.8%. The successful implementation of the proposed optimization technique for the gradient boosting classifier and also the high accuracy and robustness of the applied MLP regressor model for modeling of the considered water quality parameters verify reliability, validity and applicability of the proposed methodologies for real time assessment of water quality as well as the monitoring and management of water and environment resources effectively.
